text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
METASTATIC HORMONE-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER
12-Month On-Treatment Assessment

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [GU Oncology Attending]
DEPARTMENT: Genitourinary Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Routine Surveillance and Treatment Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, presenting for his 12-month on-treatment follow-up visit while on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate/prednisone. He presents today for routine PSA and testosterone monitoring, disease status assessment, and ongoing toxicity management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male — correction, 69-year-old male — with a diagnosis of stage IV metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma established approximately 12 months ago. At the time of initial diagnosis, his PSA was markedly elevated at 187 ng/mL. Staging workup at that time demonstrated extensive osseous metastatic disease involving the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck, as well as a single right obturator lymph node measuring 1.2 cm on CT. There was no evidence of visceral metastases at diagnosis. Biopsy demonstrated high-grade adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with perineural invasion and androgen receptor-positive immunophenotype, without neuroendocrine differentiation. Germline testing performed at diagnosis revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with a hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome pedigree (his brother had pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). Somatic NGS at diagnosis further demonstrated TP53 p.R248W, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 heterozygous deletion, and MYC gain — a genomically high-risk profile consistent with his aggressive clinical phenotype.

Approximately 12 months ago, the patient was initiated on intensified systemic therapy with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months for androgen deprivation, combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally once daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily, consistent with the LATITUDE and STAMPEDE trial data supporting intensified first-line therapy in high-volume or high-risk mHSPC. Castrate testosterone levels were confirmed at 6 weeks (< 20 ng/dL). The patient had an excellent initial PSA response: PSA declined from 187 ng/mL to 3.2 ng/mL at 3 months and reached a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 6 months.

Today at his 12-month assessment, the patient reports overall reasonable tolerability of his current regimen. He notes mild, persistent fatigue that he rates as 3/10 in severity and describes as a general lack of his former energy level — he was previously active as a retired steelworker and now finds that he tires more easily with household activities. He reports no worsening of back pain and no new bone pain. He has not experienced any falls. He does endorse ongoing mild hot flashes associated with castration therapy, occurring several times daily and mildly disruptive to sleep. No cognitive complaints. No urinary symptoms of concern beyond long-standing post-void dribbling from his underlying prostate pathology. No hematuria.

With respect to the abiraterone-related side effect profile, he endorses mild ankle swelling, which has been intermittently present. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. His blood pressure was noted to be elevated at his last visit with his primary care physician and required adjustment of his antihypertensive regimen (his lisinopril dose was increased approximately 2 months ago, with good subsequent blood pressure control per his report). He has been taking his prednisone 5 mg twice daily consistently and denies symptoms of adrenal insufficiency. No GI complaints attributable to abiraterone or prednisone (no significant dyspepsia, heartburn, or nausea). His type 2 diabetes remains well-controlled per his endocrinologist; he is on metformin and his last HbA1c was 6.9%. He continues calcium 1000 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily for bone health in the setting of ongoing ADT-related bone loss.

He had restaging bone scan imaging performed approximately 6 months ago (at the 6-month mark) which was not yet available at our last encounter; those results are reviewed today and are discussed in the Imaging section below.

The patient is accompanied today by his wife, who confirms the history and expresses that he has been "doing reasonably well" though notes the fatigue is more prominent than before treatment. The family remains engaged and well-informed regarding the treatment goals (disease control, prolongation of time to castration resistance) and the palliative intent of therapy in the metastatic setting.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1), chronic and stable. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Appetite intact; stable weight (no significant unintentional weight loss).
Neurological: No headache, no focal weakness, no paresthesias. No cognitive complaints or memory changes. No dizziness.
Musculoskeletal: Back pain at baseline/minimal — no acute worsening. No new bone pain. No hip pain. No pathologic fracture symptoms.
Cardiovascular: Mild bilateral ankle edema, new/worsened compared to pre-treatment. No chest pain, no dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with vigorous effort, similar to prior. Hot flashes several times daily.
Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea at rest or with routine activity.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No right upper quadrant discomfort. Bowel function normal. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: Post-void dribbling (chronic). No hematuria. Decreased libido and erectile dysfunction consistent with ongoing castration.
Endocrine: Hot flashes as noted. No excessive thirst, polyuria beyond baseline diabetes management. No symptoms suggesting adrenal insufficiency (no unusual fatigue, hypotension, nausea, or hyponatremia symptoms).
Skin: No rash. No bruising.
Psychiatric: Mood appropriate. Wife notes he is occasionally moody, likely attributable to androgen deprivation effects. No overt depression on brief screen.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma, stage IV (T3bN1M1b), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5 — as above
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22)
3. Hypertension — on antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril, recently uptitrated)
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin; HbA1c ~6.9%
5. Hyperlipidemia — on statin therapy

SURGICAL HISTORY
- No prior prostate surgery
- No prior cancer-directed surgery
- Appendectomy in remote past

FAMILY HISTORY
- Brother: pancreatic cancer (deceased)
- Sister: breast cancer (BRCA2-associated)
- Parents: history not fully known; father with possible cardiac disease

SOCIAL HISTORY
Mr. Kowalski is a retired steelworker of Eastern European descent. He is married and lives with his wife. He has adult children who are supportive. He is a lifelong non-smoker. Denies alcohol use beyond rare social occasions. No illicit drug use. He is functionally independent with all ADLs. He was moderately active prior to his cancer diagnosis (walking, light yardwork) and continues these activities at a somewhat reduced pace due to fatigue.

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS
1. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months — last injection administered [recent date]
2. Abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on an empty stomach
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (with abiraterone, for mineralocorticoid excess prevention)
4. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily (recently uptitrated from 10 mg)
5. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (added for blood pressure optimization)
6. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily
7. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
8. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally twice daily
9. Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally once daily
10. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed for mild bone discomfort (uses infrequently)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg (improved from last visit), heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.2°F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 181 lbs (stable from prior visit), height 5'9", BMI 26.7 kg/m2.

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing, well-nourished, elderly male in no acute distress. Alert and fully oriented. Conversational and in good spirits today.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear. No cervical lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Bilateral ankle edema 1+ (mild, pitting), worse on the right.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Unlabored breathing at rest.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds. No periumbilical or right upper quadrant tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: No midline spinal tenderness on palpation of the lumbar or thoracic spine today. Normal lower extremity strength bilaterally (5/5). No focal weakness. No evidence of pathologic fracture.

Neurological: Alert, oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Normal gait. No evidence of spinal cord compromise.

Lymph Nodes: No supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy appreciable on exam. (Note: the known right obturator lymph node metastasis is not palpable on external exam.)

Skin: No rash, no jaundice, no petechiae or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 1.1 ng/mL
  — PSA nadir: 0.8 ng/mL (at 6 months)
  — PSA at baseline: 187 ng/mL
  — PSA trend: Nadir at month 6 (0.8 ng/mL), slight uptick to 1.1 ng/mL at month 12; remains well below baseline
  — This minor PSA rise from nadir does not fulfill criteria for biochemical progression at this time; no 3 consecutive rises and the absolute value remains very low

Serum Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL — confirms continued castrate state

Complete Blood Count:
  — WBC: 6.4 K/uL (normal)
  — Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mildly decreased, consistent with ADT/castration-related anemia — chronic normocytic)
  — Platelets: 218 K/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  — Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L (normal; clinically important on prednisone/abiraterone given mineralocorticoid toxicity monitoring)
  — Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73m2
  — AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 24 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/L (all within normal limits; hepatotoxicity monitoring on abiraterone — no liver function abnormalities)
  — Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
  — Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting; acceptable given T2DM on metformin)
  — Albumin: 3.8 g/dL

Lipid Panel (reviewed from recent PCP visit):
  — LDL: 88 mg/dL (well-controlled on statin)
  — HDL: 44 mg/dL
  — Triglycerides: 138 mg/dL

HbA1c (from recent PCP/endocrinology visit): 6.9% — well-controlled

Note: No evidence of mineralocorticoid excess (potassium normal, blood pressure now well-controlled after antihypertensive adjustment; no edema escalation). No hepatotoxicity on abiraterone. Mild normocytic anemia consistent with castration-related erythropoietic suppression.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging bone scan and CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed approximately 6 months ago (at the 6-month on-treatment mark) are now formally reviewed and discussed with the patient today:

Bone Scan: Previously identified lesions at the L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck now show sclerotic changes consistent with treatment response — the intensity of radiotracer uptake at these sites has notably decreased compared to the baseline scan, reflecting bone healing in the setting of effective disease control. No new foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified. The pattern is reassuring for a meaningful osseous response.

CT Abdomen/Pelvis: The right obturator lymph node has decreased in size, measuring 0.7 cm on short-axis dimension (down from 1.2 cm at staging). This represents a meaningful reduction in nodal disease burden. No new lymphadenopathy. No visceral metastases — liver and spleen unremarkable, no adrenal lesions, no new pelvic masses. The prostate gland itself is not meaningfully re-evaluated in the metastatic treatment setting.

CT Chest (included at the 6-month scan): No pulmonary nodules or parenchymal metastases. No mediastinal adenopathy. Cardiovascular silhouette within normal limits.

Overall imaging impression: Findings are consistent with partial response to ADT plus abiraterone therapy — both osseous and nodal disease have responded favorably. This is concordant with the PSA response trajectory.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with stage IV metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), harboring germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant and a genomically complex somatic profile (TP53, PTEN loss, RB1 loss, MYC gain). He is now 12 months into intensified ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone.

1. DISEASE STATUS — METASTATIC PROSTATE CANCER, RESPONDING TO THERAPY:
The patient has had an excellent response to date. PSA has declined from 187 ng/mL at baseline to a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 6 months and remains low at 1.1 ng/mL today. The marginal uptick from 0.8 to 1.1 ng/mL does not fulfill PSA progression criteria; I would characterize this as a minor fluctuation in the context of ongoing excellent disease control. Testosterone confirmed castrate (< 20 ng/dL), ensuring that the hormonal backbone remains intact. Restaging imaging from the 6-month mark demonstrates partial response in both the osseous disease (sclerotic healing changes, decreased uptake) and in the nodal compartment (right obturator node decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm). No new metastatic lesions at the 6-month scan.

Current treatment: Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next injection scheduled today/this visit), abiraterone 1000 mg orally daily on empty stomach, and prednisone 5 mg BID.

Next restaging imaging: Bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis planned in approximately 6 months (18-month on-treatment mark), or sooner if PSA rises more substantially or new symptoms develop. No PSMA PET indicated at this time given current disease control.

2. PSA MONITORING:
PSA to be rechecked in 3 months. If PSA continues to rise on 3 consecutive measurements, we will need to confirm castrate testosterone and evaluate for castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). Given this patient's genomic profile (BRCA2 germline loss, TP53, PTEN, RB1 alterations), I have a heightened level of concern about the potential for earlier-than-average development of castration resistance; I will monitor closely.

I would note proactively: in the event of future CRPC progression following abiraterone, second-line options we would consider include enzalutamide or a PARP inhibitor given the biallelic BRCA2 loss (PROfound trial data). These options will be discussed with the patient at the appropriate time.

3. HYPERTENSION — ABIRATERONE-RELATED EXACERBATION:
Blood pressure is now well-controlled at 132/78 mmHg following the uptitration of lisinopril to 20 mg and addition of amlodipine 5 mg. Continue current antihypertensive regimen. Will recheck blood pressure at next visit. Patient counseled to continue home blood pressure monitoring and follow up with PCP as needed.

4. FATIGUE — GRADE 1, ADT-RELATED:
Ongoing Grade 1 fatigue is expected in the setting of castration therapy. The patient is not anemic at a level requiring intervention (Hgb 11.6 g/dL). ADT-related fatigue is multifactorial (loss of androgens, mild anemia, sleep disruption from hot flashes). Recommendations: maintain physical activity as tolerated (light walking, resistance exercise for sarcopenia prevention and fatigue mitigation), optimize sleep hygiene, consider cognitive behavioral approaches if fatigue worsens. No dose reduction or treatment interruption warranted at this time. Hot flashes: patient declines pharmacologic intervention for vasomotor symptoms at this time (was offered venlafaxine or gabapentin); will revisit as needed.

5. MILD LOWER-EXTREMITY EDEMA — ABIRATERONE/MINERALOCORTICOID EFFECT:
Grade 1 bilateral ankle edema, consistent with known abiraterone/mineralocorticoid excess effect. Potassium normal, blood pressure now controlled. Recommends continued dietary sodium restriction. If edema worsens or is symptomatic, may add low-dose spironolactone or recommend compression stockings. Will monitor.

6. BONE HEALTH:
Patient on ongoing ADT — at significant risk for accelerated bone mineral density loss leading to osteoporosis and fracture risk, independent of osseous metastatic disease. Continue calcium 1000 mg daily (split dosing) and vitamin D 1000 IU daily. DEXA scan has not yet been performed at our center — will order a DEXA scan to establish a baseline BMD, given that this patient is now 12 months into continuous ADT. Will refer for DEXA and discuss bisphosphonate or RANK-L inhibitor therapy (e.g., denosumab 60 mg SQ every 6 months for osteoporosis prevention, separately from any future bone-metastasis dosing regimen) depending on T-score results.

7. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
HbA1c 6.9% — well-controlled. Ongoing monitoring with endocrinology/PCP. Note: prednisone can modestly elevate glucose; continue current metformin regimen. No change needed.

8. GERMLINE BRCA2 — FAMILY COUNSELING:
I have again discussed the implications of his pathogenic germline BRCA2 variant for first-degree relatives. His adult children have been offered genetic counseling referral. Proactive testing of family members recommended. This does not alter his own immediate treatment plan today.

9. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND GOALS OF CARE:
ECOG performance status 1 today. Patient is living independently, engaged with family, and maintaining reasonable quality of life. Goals of care reaffirmed: patient understands that therapy is palliative in intent and that the goal is prolonged disease control and maintenance of quality of life. No change in goals at this visit. No hospice discussion indicated at present given stable, responding disease.

RETURN TO CLINIC: 3 months for PSA and testosterone recheck, clinical assessment, and toxicity review. Sooner as needed if symptoms develop.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, more than half of which was spent counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by GU Medical Oncology Attending]
[Dictated and reviewed by GU Medical Oncology Attending]
